articles.title: The Father in His Field
articles.author: Ouida Johns Pedersen
articles.complete_text:
With gnarled and humble hands he sets the seed
And trains with patient fingers the young shoot.
He meets with constant care the spreading need
Of breaking leaf, of dark and searching root.

When winds are summer born against the flower
He channels seeping water to sustain
The growing, and awaits the stormy hour
To fight the onslaught of too sudden rain,

Until at last he holds within his palm
The amber fruit. His shoulders bent and thin
Are grown erect by visions of new birth.
And now his silent lips will phrase a psalm
Of gratitude. The cycle will begin
Once more with kernels for the spring's brown earth.
articles.notes: This is a complete poem that appears to be the frontispiece for the issue, as indicated by the text 'Frontispiece: Mount Timpanogos, Utah' reference

articles.title: No Wonder We Love Him
articles.author: Edith Smith Elliott
articles.complete_text:
WHEN we contemplate the widespread meaning of the six-letter word father, we are somewhat astonished. Aside from the dictionary definition, its use has encompassed conditions, movements, and institutions. In feudal times the lords and barons had paternalistic authority over their vassals. Many religious groups use the word father for those who teach or counsel their members. The term founding fathers has been used for those whose contributions form the framework of institutions whether political, economic, or religious. We refer to George Washington as the "Father of our Country." We honor him for his foresight in establishing democratic principles that have lasted in our country since its infancy. Thomas Jefferson was the "Father of the Declaration of Independence," and James Madison the "Father of the United States Constitution." To the Negro, Abraham Lincoln is the "Father of Freedom." Simon Bolivar and Jose de San Martin were the "Revolutionary Fathers" of South America who were responsible for freeing their countries from Europe.

Most political groups have a filial feeling toward their founders and organizers. The different inventors are given the title of father of their invention. Examples are the Wright Brothers, fathers of the airplane; Alexander Graham Bell, father of the telephone; and Eli Whitney, father of the cotton gin.

Eminent scientists...
articles.notes: This appears to be the beginning of a substantial article about fathers and fatherhood by Edith Smith Elliott, who is identified as a member of the Relief Society General Board and daughter of President George Albert Smith. The text cuts off but shows clear article structure.

misc_summary: The remaining content consists of advertisements (Sego Milk, nylon skirts), reader letters/testimonials, Relief Society General Board member listings, magazine masthead information, table of contents, and subscription/publishing details.
